items.q: Does balancing an equation make the number of molecules equal on both sides?
items.correct: No — it makes the number of atoms of each element equal on both sides; the molecule counts can differ.
items.misconception: A balanced equation must have the same number of molecules on each side.
items.random_wrong: Balancing means changing the formulae of compounds until they match.
items.why: Students focus on the big coefficient numbers rather than counting atoms of each element, which is what conservation of mass requires.

items.q: If you double the volume of a solution at the same concentration, what happens to the moles of solute?
items.correct: They double — n = c × V, so at fixed concentration the amount scales with volume.
items.misconception: The moles stay the same because the concentration has not changed.
items.random_wrong: The moles halve because the solute is more spread out.
items.why: Students treat concentration and amount as the same quantity, instead of concentration being amount per unit volume.

items.q: Can a percentage yield be greater than 100%?
items.correct: No — an apparent yield over 100% signals an error, such as the product being wet or impure.
items.misconception: A yield over 100% means the reaction worked better than expected.
items.random_wrong: Yields over 100% occur whenever a catalyst is used.
items.why: Students forget the theoretical yield is the absolute maximum fixed by the limiting reactant; you cannot create extra atoms.

items.q: How many moles of NaOH are in 25.0 cm³ of 0.100 mol dm⁻³ solution?
items.why: It's 2.50 × 10⁻³ mol. “2.50 mol” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, how many moles of water form from 3 mol of H₂ with excess oxygen?
items.why: It's 3 mol. “1.5 mol” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Calculate the mass of 0.250 mol of calcium carbonate, CaCO₃ (Mr = 100.1).
items.why: Exact answer: 25.0 g. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
